FULL CONTENT:
[![Penn State Materials Research Institute](/sites/default/files/inline-images/PSU_MRI_RGB_REV_2C.png)](https://www.mri.psu.edu)

![Penn State Materials Research Institute](/sites/default/files/inline-images/PSU_MRI_RGB_REV_2C.png)

# 'Highly perfect': Meeting the challenge of making 2D semiconductor materials

![Redwing Group](/sites/default/files/styles/large/public/inline-news-images/Joan%20Redwing.jpg?itok=i8VTx8bR "Redwing research group in the 2DCC-MIP labs. Credit: Seana Wood/Penn State MRI")

#### Redwing research group in the 2DCC-MIP labs. Credit: Seana Wood/Penn State MRI

By Jamie Oberdick

Two-dimensional materials are vital for the type of semiconductors that will push the future of electronic devices and energy-efficient lighting, but they are a challenge to make. They must have very few defects, difficult given their very small, nano-level size.

Helping Penn State pull off the synthesis of these hard-to-produce materials is Joan Redwing, director of the National Science Foundation-sponsored Two-Dimensional Crystal Consortium (2DCC) – Materials Innovation Platform, and distinguished professor of materials science and engineering and electrical engineering. In these roles, Redwing plays a key part in Penn State’s semiconductor research.

“I work on techniques for making thin films of semiconductors, along with making nano structures like nano wires and two-dimensional films which are also very small in size,” Redwing said. “My research focuses on understanding these processes at an in-depth level so that we can control the properties of the semiconductor material, which is vital because the semiconductor needs to be highly perfect, with no missing atoms or other defects. I study how to control the synthesis of materials at that level so that we can achieve highly perfect films that then are used in electronic and optical devices.”

Redwing and the 2DCC’s work in synthesizing semiconductor materials is a practical benefit for other semiconductor researchers, as they make the difficult-to-create materials that drive semiconductor research forward.

"The materials that my group makes are used by a variety of other researchers at Penn State, who are working on device fabrication, doing characterization of the materials or process development,” Redwing said. "My role is to advance the materials themselves; to develop new materials and structures and to improve the quality of existing materials that we have in support of making better semiconductor devices.”

This is not limited to Penn State. The 2DCC not only provides these materials to Penn State researchers but works with semiconductor experts from industry and higher education institutions beyond Penn State.

"The 2DCC is a national user facility funded by the National Science Foundation,” Redwing said. "That means we have researchers from outside of Penn State who either use the materials that we make, or they come on site to make materials for their own research. So, that is a big way that we collaborate with researchers outside of Penn State.”

Semiconductors are Redwing’s life work. She began her career researching the semiconductor material gallium nitride, playing a part in the development of these materials for use in blue-light emitting diodes which is the basis for all of today’s LED lighting. Currently, one of Redwing’s specialties is the main manufacturing technique for making gallium nitride materials for device application – metalorganic chemical vapor deposition.

The ubiquitous use of LED lights in so many applications makes gallium nitride an important semiconductor material, similar to silicon.

“LEDs have so many uses, more than just lighting your house or as decorative lighting," Redwing said. “For example, they are used in backlighting of displays in cell phones and laptops which has enabled the displays to become much thinner and lighter weight than in the past. These are just a few of the tremendous number of ways in which developing new semiconductor materials like gallium nitride have enabled us to have new devices and new applications that we just would not have otherwise.”

Redwing notes that many of the students who have passed through her research group have moved on to semiconductor-related careers, many of them with Intel. Given that semiconductors are only becoming more important as applications such as the growing use of smart devices, they provide a variety of employment opportunities that will only increase with the CHIPS Act becoming law.

“I am really excited about the CHIPS Act passing because this is going to give my students even more opportunities in the future,” Redwing said. “A lot of the U.S. semiconductor companies were not only doing manufacturing abroad but also a lot of their R&D, so my students were losing job opportunities in the U.S. I am happy that the U.S. is making this investment. The public should understand that semiconductors are as important, if not even more important, for our society’s future as oil and gas are now.”

*This article originally appeared in the Fall 2022 issue of Focus on Materials.*

--- SOURCE 4: Recent progress, challenges, and opportunities in 2D ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1748013224001117

SUMMARY:
This review summarizes the most recent developments in scalable synthesis of various 2D materials and their application in flexible displays. Next, a detailed synopsis is provided on the state of the art and potential utilization of different 2D materials in flexible emissive films, encapsulation layers, thin film transistors and flexible transparent electrodes for OLED displays. This study primarily presents the state of the art and potential applications of these 2D materials in various components of flexible displays, such as emissive layers, TFTs, encapsulation layers, and transparent conducting electrodes, as shown in Fig. 1. Various 2D materials with diverse properties exhibit different pros and cons for application in flexible displays.

FULL CONTENT:

Review

Recent progress, challenges, and opportunities in 2D materials for flexible displays
============================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Aumber Abbas a b, Yingjie Luo c, Waqas Ahmad g, Mujahid Mustaqeem d, Lingan Kong a, Jiwei Chen c, Guigang Zhou a, Tanveer A.Tabish e, Qian Zhang f, Qijie Liang a

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nantod.2024.102256](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nantod.2024.102256 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1748013224001117&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •Flexible displays are emerging trends in electronics for innovative designs. 
*   •Key 2D materials offer remarkable properties for flexible displays. 
*   •Recent research focuses on 2D materials with multifaceted properties. 
*   •Challenges in 2D materials include uniformity, scalability, stability and flexibility. 
*   •Future research aims to commercialize 2D materials for advanced flexible displays. 

Abstract
--------

Emerging trends in flexible electronics have led to the creation of novel foldable, rollable, and stretchy displays to maintain their functionality under diverse deformations. However, in order to produce flexible devices, it is necessary to develop [novel materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/novel-material "Learn more about novel materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with desired metallic, semiconducting, or insulating properties while maintaining flexibility. Emerging two-dimensional materials like hexagonal boron [nitride](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nitride "Learn more about nitride from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), graphene, and [transition metal dichalcogenides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/transition-metal-dichalcogenide "Learn more about transition metal dichalcogenides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have recently received a lot of scientific interest due to their remarkable optical, electrical, and [mechanical characteristics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-property "Learn more about mechanical characteristics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which make them ideal for flexible electronics. This review summarizes the most recent developments in scalable synthesis of various [2D materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/2d-material "Learn more about 2D materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their application in flexible displays. Initially, the rising demand for flexible organic light-emitting diode (OLED) displays is discussed. Next, a detailed synopsis is provided on the state of the art and potential utilization of different [2D materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/2d-material "Learn more about 2D materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in flexible emissive films, encapsulation layers, [thin film transistors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thin-film-transistor "Learn more about thin film transistors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and flexible transparent electrodes for OLED displays. Then, a comparison of advantages and disadvantages of different 2D materials for different applications is provided. Finally, the challenges encountered, possible solutions and perspective future research directions are furnished for exploring 2D materials in advanced flexible display applications.

------------------

2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1748013224001117-ga1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Introduction
------------

The competition for gadgets with high resolution and smart features has substantially boosted the display market. The most common technology on the market is liquid crystal displays (LCDs). However, organic light-emitting diodes (OLED) displays are now gaining higher demand because they can be used to make novel sleek designs with fewer parts than LCDs [1]. OLED displays are a strong competitor for next generation displays that are smaller, more curved, and more flexible [2]. LCDs can reach a reduced profile by attaching backlight to the edges, but they are hampered by problems with poor brightness and limited color reproduction.

OLEDs possess specific distinguishing characteristics that differentiate them from traditional light-emitting diodes (LEDs), regardless of their common operational mechanism. OLEDs consist of organic semiconductor materials that exhibit electroluminescent properties upon the application of voltage. In contrast, LEDs are composed of inorganic semiconductors and require the use of backlights. Moreover, OLED displays possess a broader viewing angle (84°), reduced weight, faster response times, and consume less electrical energy compared to LEDs due to their inherent properties [3]. Therefore, the utilization of organic semiconductors in OLEDs represents a significant progression in optoelectronic technology, surpassing the capabilities of LEDs.

OLED displays are manufactured through the assembly of a multi-layer structure consisting of various advanced materials, each of which is carefully designed for its intended purpose. The roles of charge transport, charge injection, and light emission are fulfilled by these layers [4]. The central objective of OLED display technology is to accurately control and manipulate the electric current that passes through individual pixels within the display. OLEDs can be classified as current-controlled devices, wherein the luminosity of the emitted light is directly proportional to the magnitude of the electric current passing through them. The arrangement of pixels in a matrix format is employed to enhance the visual representation of images or textual content. Multiple types of OLEDs are commercially employed for different applications, and their classification is determined by their operational principle and structural characteristics [3], as presented in Table 1.

Unfortunately, most of the traditional OLED displays lack elasticity and are inflexible because they are made of fragile, unstable materials that break easily when twisted or stretched. Therefore, researchers have recently devoted significant efforts to explore novel materials for stretchable and flexible OLED displays.

New design opportunities for cutting-edge smartphones and laptops have become available with the development of foldable screens. The first foldable OLED smartphone with folding screens was announced by Samsung Electronics in 2018 and officially released in 2019 [5], [6]. Samsung Display supplied the flexible displays for these devices. Next, laptops with foldable OLED panels from LG Display were shown by Lenovo and Hewlett-Packard in 2020 and 2022, respectively [7], [8]. Since then, a number of firms, including Huawei, Motorola, and others, have released foldable handsets for sale in response to customer demand for cutting-edge display technology. Moreover, there is growing interest in wearable flexible OLED displays due to their ability to maintain high brightness even when subjected to mechanical stresses or deformations. These features make them ideal for a wide variety of applications in wearable electronics, including health monitoring, biomedical devices, electronic skins, and human-machine interfaces (e. g., robotics). Thus, flexible wearable displays are anticipated to be accessible in the not-too-distant future because of the drive for innovation in displays that has boosted technical developments.

In order to create flexible screens, flexible materials are essential. Reduced bending stress caused by thinner materials can also increase the display's durability. Therefore, one of the biggest challenges in the advancement of OLED display technology is finding thin and flexible materials to replace the traditional ones. Atomically thin 2D materials have drawn a lot of interest in both science and business over the last ten years because of their exceptional electrical conductivity, durability, and optical clarity.

This study focuses on the recent advancements in 2D materials for flexible displays. Graphene is a potential candidate for transparent electrodes due to its robust mechanical properties, high light transmittance and fast charge transport. Thin-film transistor (TFT) applications may benefit from the fabrication of semiconductor channels from transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) with semiconducting properties. As a result of their direct bandgap, monolayer TMDs may also be used as light-emitting layers. While insulating hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) may be employed for encapsulating and insulating layers in displays due to its exceptional thermal conductivity and chemical stability. This study primarily presents the state of the art and potential applications of these 2D materials in various components of flexible displays, such as emissive layers, TFTs, encapsulation layers, and transparent conducting electrodes, as shown in Fig. 1.

Section snippets
----------------

Chemical vapor deposition
-------------------------

The most popular process extensively used in industry is chemical vapor deposition (CVD), which makes it possible to fabricate high-quality thin films on a large scale. Since 2009 [9], 2D materials such as h-BN, graphene, and TMDs have been grown via CVD and are now the subject of ongoing study. However, due to the defects generated during the growth process, the characteristics of CVD-grown 2D materials differ from those of exfoliated flakes and theoretical predictions. It is crucial to

Flexible emissive films
-----------------------

Emissive layers play an important role in OLEDs, where the creation of excitons and recombination of the charge carriers takes place and light is produced. Therefore, the emissive layer is the heart of an OLED device. To fabricate stable, high-efficiency OLED device that emit photons of the desired color, the emitter must have high external quantum efficiency (EQE). The thin films of organic materials such as quinacrine and acridine orange were first employed in 1950 s to generate

Encapsulation layer
-------------------

Encapsulation is a critical technique in the display industry for shielding OLEDs from oxygen and moisture. Moisture blocking is necessary to prevent damage at water vapor transmission rates (WVTR) of 10−6 g m−2 day−1 [61], which is equal to one water drop per second on a soccer field that is 100 m long and 50 m broad for one month. Traditional encapsulation techniques involved the application of a glass lid on the substrate using a low-permeation epoxy. However, the flexible OLED displays

Flexible transistors
--------------------

A transistor is a semiconductor-based electrical component that controls the direction of current flow. A thin layer of a material is used to create flexible thin film transistors (TFTs), a particular kind of transistor. TFTs are used in displays to regulate the illumination of the pixels that make up the screen. Blue, green, and red (B, G, and R) subpixels make up each pixel, and they all need current to produce color. Each subpixel has a TFT, which drives the appropriate current to the pixel

Flexible transparent electrodes
-------------------------------

Transparent electrodes are essential for creating transparent and flexible electronics, but they also have to adhere to strict conductivity and durability standards. A transparent electrode specifically must have a sheet resistance of 500 Ω sq−1 or less and a visible light transmittance of 85% or greater. The gadget must meet these requirements in order to be both transparent and conductive as well as strong enough to bear mechanical stress. It may be difficult to meet these criteria, but

Advantages and disadvantages of different 2D materials
------------------------------------------------------

Various 2D materials with diverse properties exhibit different pros and cons for application in flexible displays. A comparison of the various properties of graphene, TMDs, MXenes, perovskites, and h-BN for flexible display applications is provided in Table 4. Each material's electrical conductivity, mechanical flexibility, transparency, bandgap, synthesis scalability, environmental stability, thermal conductivity, and toxicity are evaluated. Conclusion and outlook
----------------------

We have discussed how 2D materials may be used in display technologies. Instead of using conventional materials, 2D materials are the best choice for flexible displays due to their outstanding mechanical properties and atomic-level thinness. Furthermore, 2D materials have special electrical characteristics that make them appropriate for a variety of display components, including transparent conducting electrodes, encapsulation layers, TFTs, and emissive layers. *   ### [A machine learning-quantum chemical quest to predict the photovoltaic properties of 2D molybdenum sulfide super structures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0925346725004161)

2025, Optical Materials  Show abstract The current study introduces a machine learning (ML)-driven method to forecast the photovoltaic (PV) characteristics of two-dimensional (2D) molybdenum sulfide (MoS) superstructures. Structural data of about 1202 compounds is compiled and four top performing models are trained to predict their open-circuit voltage (V oc). The models are evaluated by using their mean squared errors (MSE), which spanned from 0.47 to 1.23, reflecting adequate predictive accuracy. Their stability evaluations are determined via their tolerance factor which reveals a significant relationship with both V oc. Moreover, the materials with its optical properties are confirmed using the Kramers-Kronig relations, displaying a high level of consistency between experimental data and simulated results. The SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis identifies that their polarizability (Alpha) and first order connectivity (chi1) are the most important factors to affect their model performance. The bandgaps of MoS 2 and MoS 33 are calculated as 1.47 and 0.72 eV, respectively. These results can enhance the understanding of the PV potential of MoS based 2D materials and can lay the foundation for further studies in this field.    
*   ### [Enhancing light out-coupling in flexible organic light emitting devices through the localized surface plasmon resonance of urchin-like gold nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002223132400437X)

2025, Journal of Luminescence Citation Excerpt : 
Flexible organic light emitting devices (FOLED) have received a lot of attention in academia and industry because of their potential application in wearable devices and flexible display devices [1–3].   Show abstract The local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect and scattering properties of gold nanoparticles are effective ways to improve the light extraction efficiency of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs). Here five different kinds of urchin-like gold nanoparticles (UGNs) were synthesized using the seed-mediated method. The finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method was used to calculate the near-field local and far-field scattering properties of various sea urchin-like gold nanoparticles. All five types of UGNs demonstrated certain levels of light enhancement within the visible band. The flexible OLED devices were created by doping five different types of nanoparticles into the flexible substrates. The hollow UGNs device exhibited a maximum current efficiency of 124.25 cd/A, a maximum power efficiency of 131.27 lm/W, and a maximum EQE of 45.54 %. When compared to undoped flexible devices, the maximum current efficiency, the maximum power efficiency, and the maximum EQE increase by 132 %, 82 %, and 36%, respectively. The results indicate that combining the flexible conductive substrate and UGNs is an effective strategy for improving light extraction from flexible OLEDs. This enhancement effect originates from UGNs acting as a scattering layer to reduce total light reflection, and the luminous intensity of the device is improved due to the LSPR of sea urchin-like nanoparticles.

Show abstract Organic electrochromic conducting polymers (CPs) are at the forefront of flexible and ultra-thin display electronics. Solution processable poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) exhibits excellent electrical and electrochemical behavior in line with great processability, stability, and other promising properties. enriched_points.expanded_content: Achieving high-quality 2D materials is challenging due to defects during synthesis. Ensuring uniformity and crystalline perfection is crucial for their application in advanced electronics.
Scalable synthesis methods like chemical vapor deposition (CVD) are essential, yet they often struggle to maintain quality across large areas, limiting industrial production capabilities.
Developing cost-effective synthesis techniques is vital for commercialization. High production costs hinder the widespread adoption of 2D materials in consumer electronics and other applications.
